title: Evolution of Hypoequilibrium States in Steepest Entropy Ascent Models for Nonequilibrium Quantum Thermodynamics
authors: Gian Paolo Beretta, Rohit Kishan Ray, Michael R. von Spakovsky
abstract: A formal development of the hypoequilibrium (HE) state concept within the Steepest-Entropy-Ascent Quantum Thermodynamics (SEAQT) framework is presented, emphasizing its rigorous mathematical formulation. Using a general decomposition of the Hilbert space, HE states are defined in operator language and the reduced evolution of the associated intensive parameters for the regime where the dissipative dynamics commutes with the Hamiltonian is derived. It is proved that the $M$-th order HE family (where $M$ is the number of spectral sectors) constitutes an invariant manifold under the SEAQT equation of motion, ensuring that states initially representing a ``mixture of canonicals'' maintain this structure throughout their evolution. Furthermore, a formal connection is established between the HE ansatz and the rate-controlled constrained equilibrium (RCCE) method, identifying HE variables as constraint potentials. Finally, the model is extended to non-Hamiltonian SEAQT (NH-SEAQT) interactions to describe thermodynamically consistent energy and entropy exchanges between subsystems and heat baths. This work provides the formal foundation for reduced-order modeling of far-from-equilibrium relaxation and transport processes, and supports a methodology previously applied across various physical and chemical systems.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for hypoequilibrium states within the SEAQT framework of quantum thermodynamics. It proves that these states form an invariant manifold, meaning a mixture of canonical states remains such a mixture during evolution. The work also bridges the gap between SEAQT and the RCCE method for modeling complex relaxation processes.
main_problem: The paper seeks to establish a mathematically rigorous framework for 'hypoequilibrium' (HE) states within the Steepest-Entropy-Ascent Quantum Thermodynamics (SEAQT) framework and to prove the dynamical consistency of these states.
main_result: The authors prove that the M-th order HE family constitutes an invariant manifold under SEAQT evolution, ensuring that states representing a mixture of canonicals maintain this structure. They also establish a formal connection between the HE ansatz and the Rate-Controlled Constrained-Equilibrium (RCCE) method.
method: The study uses operator-based mathematical formulations, Hilbert space decomposition into spectral sectors, and variational principles to derive reduced-order evolution equations for intensive parameters.
model_or_system: The model focuses on unstructured quantum systems governed by the SEAQT equation of motion, which augments the von Neumann equation with a nonlinear dissipative term. It also extends to non-Hamiltonian (NH-SEAQT) interactions for system-bath energy and entropy exchange.
key_observables: Entropy production rate, sector-specific temperatures (inverse temperatures), chemical potentials, sector probabilities, and the Massieu operator.
important_parameters: Spectral-sector temperatures (beta_K), sector probabilities (p_K), relaxation times (tau_K), and the regime where dissipative dynamics commute with the Hamiltonian.
main_assumptions: The primary approximation is the Hypoequilibrium (HE) assumption, which assumes rapid probability redistribution within energy sectors (HESS) and a decomposition of the Hilbert space into coarse-grained spectral sectors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the rapid relaxation of a HESS state toward a maximal-entropy state on an energy-vs-entropy diagram and shows the overall system evolution.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the formal definition of HE states, moves to the derivation of reduced evolution for intensive parameters, provides a proof of manifold invariance, establishes the link to the RCCE method, and concludes with an extension to non-Hamiltonian system-bath interactions.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a rigorous foundation for reduced-order modeling of far-from-equilibrium relaxation and transport processes using a thermodynamically consistent framework.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
